posts.name: Peter Moy
posts.cooked:
Bricking throughout. Intense bouquet of damp earth, cherries, rose petals and and a hint of violets. Absolutely captivating nose. Medium bodied and balanced, the Chambolle elegance and delicacy is on full display here. Flavors of red fruit and tangerine peel stain the palate and linger on a long, silky finish. I have never drunk a villages level wine with this much concentration and length. If I remember correctly, Roumier used to include some premier cru Cras in his Chambolle Musigny. Regardless, this is a spellbinding wine, and further evidence that villages level wines with decades of age can be very special.

I found this bottle, in of all places, in a duty free shop in Arlanda Airport, Sweden, last month. The clerk didn’t know how the bottle got on the shelf as they usually offer only the most current vintages. As the bottle didn’t have a price tag on it, he sold it to me for the equivalent of 25 euros.

posts.name: Blake R Maso
posts.cooked: Wow, what a find! If you could only see my jealous face right now

posts.name: Robert Pavlovich
posts.cooked: That’s a sweet find. As for the 1er juice going in, there is a small portion of Fuees that is still going in to the Chambolle, though I’d love to know how much. Also curious about the Cras.

posts.name: Soren R Nielsen
posts.cooked:
Great note.
Love these older burgs. -And what a nice deal you landed … Systembolaget involved, or is the duty free, a separate operation ?..

Regards, Søren.

posts.name: Stuart BeauneHead Niemtzow
posts.cooked:
Robert Pavlovich:

That’s a sweet find. As for the 1er juice going in, there is a small portion of Fuees that is still going in to the Chambolle, though I’d love to know how much. Also curious about the Cras.

ht


What are you “curious” about re: the Cras?

The Roumiers (Christophe started around 1982-3) used to put in the villages various 1er crus that they had too little of to bottle separately or the vines were too young to bottle separately. When the vines qualified they bottled them separately, like the Cras around 1992-3 and the Combettes around 2005. The 1990 was a great villages vintage; it had the nearly-ready for primetime Cras holdings in it.

What are you “curious” about re: the Cras?

The Roumiers (Christophe started around 1982-3) used to put in the villages various 1er crus that they had too little of to bottle separately or the vines were too young to bottle separately. When the vines qualified they bottled them separately, like the Cras around 1992-3 and the Combettes around 2005. The 1990 was a great villages vintage; it had the nearly-ready for primetime Cras holdings in it.

Thank you Stewart…you answered my question. Any idea about how much Fuees goes into the village wine these days?

posts.name: Stuart BeauneHead Niemtzow
posts.cooked:
No idea, Robert. Sorry. I lost touch with those things when I stopped buying wine, in 2007.

The Roumier estate is unusual, in the sense that it is owned by a large family who all have shares. (Christophe’s sister, Delphine, once showed me a photo of a “meeting” of shareholders (and she and Christophe are part of a similar thing on their mother’s side, the Ponnelle? family. I met a couple shareholders from that; I know the Corton Charlemagne comes from his mother’s family and , I think, the Charmes. Cousins are continually taking properties away from the corportation…I know Herve did. And, then Christophe, as manager, has to then replace the missing volume, so I assume he either rents or buys plots to farm. Fuees is pretty recent, to replace the Combettes, I think.

Pretty complicated stuff…as all “regional” wines can be, as so much is allowed to be put in them, for various reasons. I prefer wines I can know what’s in them, frankly, even with the regional wines (villages, bourgognes.)

posts.name: Jeremy Holmes
posts.cooked: This is probably the best village red Burg I have had Peter. What a steal at the price!

posts.name: Kent Comley
posts.cooked: Clerk missed a zero. Great story and score.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
What a great find! Sounds fantastic.

Still remember a '59 “village” Chambolle bottled by Corney & Barrow which was one of my Burgundy ‘conversion experiences’. A lot of higher appellation juice clearly went into those cuvées, plus old vines and classical winemaking.
